Name: information_theoretic_model_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of neural network compression can be characterized using rate-distortion theory, and that these limits can be used to develop more efficient compression algorithms that balance the tradeoff between compression ratio and model performance.
Related Work: Previous work has explored the application of rate-distortion theory to neural network compression, but has largely focused on empirical evaluations and heuristic methods. In contrast, our approach seeks to establish a rigorous information-theoretic foundation for neural network compression, building on recent advances in rate-distortion theory and its application to machine learning. Notable works in this area include the use of rate-distortion theory to justify model pruning (Isik et al., 2021) and the development of empirical sandwich bounds on the rate-distortion function (Yang et al., 2021).
Abstract: Neural network compression is a critical component of efficient deep learning, but the fundamental limits of compression are not well understood. In this work, we apply rate-distortion theory to characterize the tradeoff between compression ratio and model performance. We derive achievability and converse bounds on the rate-distortion function, and demonstrate how these bounds can be used to develop more efficient compression algorithms. Our approach provides a rigorous information-theoretic foundation for neural network compression, and has the potential to enable significant improvements in compression efficiency.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments to evaluate the performance of our compression algorithm on a range of benchmark datasets, including CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. We will compare our approach to existing compression methods, including model pruning and quantization, and evaluate the tradeoff between compression ratio and model performance. Specifically, we will use the following evaluation metrics: (1) compression ratio, (2) model accuracy, and (3) rate-distortion curve. We will also analyze the computational complexity of our approach and compare it to existing methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that our approach may not be directly applicable to all types of neural networks or compression scenarios. Additionally, the development of efficient algorithms for computing the rate-distortion function may be challenging. However, we believe that our approach has the potential to provide significant improvements in compression efficiency, and that the benefits outweigh the risks.
